items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.q: What does standard theory predict about a minimum wage?
items.correct: If above equilibrium, it raises wages for those still employed but causes unemployment by reducing labour demand — though empirical evidence is mixed.
items.misconception: A minimum wage always reduces unemployment because workers have more money to spend.
items.random_wrong: A minimum wage is paid by the government.
items.why: Students reverse the standard prediction. Empirical evidence is genuinely debated, but the theoretical baseline is that above-equilibrium price floors create surplus (unemployment in labour markets).

items.q: Which is NOT a factor of production?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Money. “Land” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: The whole economy. “The price of bread” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many factors of production are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 factors. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
